prompt:
Game night at the office is becoming a thing and I want a tic-tac-toe we can play in the terminal: one file, tictactoe.py, stdlib only, works over a piped stdin so I can also script it.

Board is cells 0-8, left to right, top to bottom. The module must export three functions besides the CLI (our tooling imports them): `winner(board)` takes a 9-char string of 'X'/'O'/'.' and returns 'X' or 'O' if that side has three in a row, else None. `render(board)` returns exactly three rows of cells joined with '|', with a '-+-+-' line between rows (empty cells stay '.'). `best_move(board, player)` returns the cell a PERFECT player would take: full minimax, where winning beats drawing beats losing, a faster win beats a slower one, a slower loss beats a faster one, and any remaining tie goes to the LOWEST cell index. That tie-break is a hard requirement — the bot's choices must be reproducible move for move.

CLI, two-player mode (no flags): print the board, then prompt the side to move with 'X> ' or 'O> ' (trailing space, no newline) and read a cell number from stdin. After every applied move print the board again. Anything that isn't a legal free cell 0-8 — occupied, out of range, not a number — prints 'invalid move' and re-prompts the SAME player (the board prints again before the re-prompt, same as any other turn). When someone has three in a row print 'X wins' or 'O wins' right after the final board; a full board with no winner prints 'draw'. Exit 0. If stdin runs dry mid-game just exit 0 quietly.

With --bot, X is the human and O is the bot: on O's turn there is no prompt — print 'O plays N' (N = the cell from best_move) and then the board after its move. The bot must never lose a game, no matter what the human does.

test_tictactoe.py is the acceptance contract — python3 test_tictactoe.py must pass and you don't touch it. Fair warning: it doesn't just spot-check, it walks every possible opponent line against the bot on both sides, so a merely-decent heuristic will not survive.
